coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excitation-dependent room temperature phosphorescence for multi-level anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Jinkun
Mao, Xiang
Zhao, Xia
Xie, Ting
Hu, Hao
Li, Zequan
Gao, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have received widespread attention due to their superior photoluminescence effect, high chemical stability and other characteristics. This research aims to explore a new multi-level anti-counterfeiting technology, which achieves efficient anti-counterfeiting of products by introducing the characteristics of Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) to excite room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP). Firstly, N-CDs were successfully prepared through a simple and effective synthesis method. The obtained N-CDs exhibited excellent RTP properties that lasted for 4 s, providing a feasible fluorescence (FL) label for multi-level anti-counterfeiting. Secondly, through the mixture of N-CDs and NaOH, a unique optical effect can be formed and the lifetime of FL can last for 8 ns, which improved the anti-counterfeiting capability and accuracy. Finally, further research found that the unique properties of N-CDs not only have broad application potential in the field of product anti-counterfeiting, but also can promote the development of anti-counterfeiting technology. This technique has the advantage of being easy to operate and low cost, as well as allowing RTP emission. Therefore, the multi-level anti-counterfeiting technology based on N-CDs proposed in this study provides an innovative and feasible solution to improve product security, and provides a solid foundation for research and application in related fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Excitation dependent
Room temperature phosphorescence
Multi-level anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization for N-CDs. (a) TEM and HRTEM images of N-CDs. (b) The particle size distribution of N-CDs. (c) XRD patterns of N-CDs and N-CDs@NaOH. (d) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs@NaOH. (e) Total spectra of XPS. (f) C1s spectra. (g) N1s spectra. (h) O1s spectra. (i) Raman for N-CDs.

The FL and RTP photos of N-CDs@NaOH samples and the FL, RTP and UV–visible absorption spectra of each sample. (a) FL and RTP photos of N-CDs@NaOH samples under 254 nm and 365 nm excitation. (b) FL spectra of N-CDs solution. (c) UV, excitation and FL emission spectra of N-CDs under 365 nm excitation. (d) The excitation spectra of N-CDs@NaOH samples and the emission FL spectra under 254 nm and 365 nm excitation. (e) The excitation spectra of N-CDs@NaOH samples and the RTP emission spectra under 254 nm and 365 nm excitation. (f) Three-dimensional FL spectra of N-CDs solution. (g) Three-dimensional FL spectra of N-CDs@NaOH samples. (h) FL of N-CDs in solutions of different polarities.

Synthesis and characterization for CDs. (a) FL spectra of CDs solution. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, excitation and FL emission spectra of CDs under 365 nm excitation. (c) Three-dimensional FL spectra of CDs. (d) RTP photo of CDs@NaOH sample. (e) Total spectra of XPS. (f) C1s spectra. (g) O1s spectra.

The RTP lifetime of N-CDs and N-CDs@NaOH samples and the mechanism of RTP generation. (a) FL lifetime of N-CDs. (b) RTP lifetime of N-CDs@NaOH. (c) Synthesis of N-CDs@NaOH. (d) FL and RTP generation mechanism of the sample.
Anti-counterfeiting and information encryption application of N-CDs@NaOH sample. (a) Anti-counterfeiting applications. (b) Information encryption applications.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excitation-dependent room temperature phosphorescence for multi-level anti-counterfeiting applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources, Environment and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have received widespread attention due to their superior photoluminescence effect, high chemical stability and other characteristics. This research aims to explore a new multi-level anti-counterfeiting technology, which achieves efficient anti-counterfeiting of products by introducing the characteristics of Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) to excite room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP). Firstly, N-CDs were successfully prepared through a simple and effective synthesis method. The obtained N-CDs exhibited excellent RTP properties that lasted for 4 s, providing a feasible fluorescence (FL) label for multi-level anti-counterfeiting. Secondly, through the mixture of N-CDs and NaOH, a unique optical effect can be formed and the lifetime of FL can last for 8 ns, which improved the anti-counterfeiting capability and accuracy. Finally, further research found that the unique properties of N-CDs not only have broad application potential in the field of product anti-counterfeiting, but also can promote the development of anti-counterfeiting technology. This technique has the advantage of being easy to operate and low cost, as well as allowing RTP emission. Therefore, the multi-level anti-counterfeiting technology based on N-CDs proposed in this study provides an innovative and feasible solution to improve product security, and provides a solid foundation for research and application in related fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anti-counterfeiting has always been a key topic that people pay attention to, and anti-counterfeiting materials have also followed the development of the times and gradually moved towards low-cost, high-efficiency and environmentally friendly directions [1,2]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging environmentally friendly material, have been widely used in many fields due to their excellent stability, good biocompatibility, large-scale synthesis and excellent luminescence effect [3–7].

However, the application effect of traditional excitation-dependent CDs in anti-counterfeiting is not very ideal, and they are deficient in preventing counterfeiting and effects of anti-counterfeiting. From a practical application perspective, the development of advanced multi-level anti-counterfeiting materials is urgently needed but remains a huge challenge. To this end, researchers have recently designed some organic afterglow materials with stimulus-responsive effects by using crystallization engineering strategies to adjust molecular conformation and packing [8–10]. This material not only has long luminescence lifetime and high signal-to-noise ratio, but also eliminates short-lived background fluorescence (FL) [11]. At the same time, changes in the RTP duration of materials under external stimulation can also be used as visual monitoring parameters to further enhance the anti-counterfeiting effect [12–14]. Therefore, stimulus-responsive afterglow materials are regarded as ideal materials for information storage, encryption, and anti-counterfeiting security protection applications [15,16].

At present, most CDs afterglow materials are prepared by embedding CDs into a matrix to form hydrogen bonds or special structures to limit their non-radiative transition effects, thereby forming the afterglow effect [16]. Han et al. [17] used silicon as a doping element to build Si–C covalent bonds within CDs to achieve the afterglow effect, and formed bridging hydrogen between the nano components of the afterglow CDs nanomaterials and water molecules after the introduction of water. The bond network therefore showed a change in the afterglow from blue to green controlled by water. Based on this change, reversible multi-level information encryption applications were realized by alternately moistening and drying. Jie et al. [18] embedded N-doped CDs (N-CDs) which was prepared from citric acid and diethanolamine into a boric acid matrix, and obtained B, N co-doped ultra-long afterglow CDs with a lifetime up to 2.02 s and a visible room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) time of 16 s. The author reduced the energy difference by co-doping B and N atoms and suppressed non-radiative decay by constructing an effective multi-constraint effect, effectively extending the afterglow lifetime. Lu et al. [19] obtained the synthesized composite by calcining green CDs blended with aluminum hydroxide (Al(OH)3). The generated hybrid material g-CDs@Al2O3 exhibited blue FL and green RTP emission under excitation at 365 nm. At the same time, this composite material can also emit yellow RTP under white light illumination. Based on this property of showing different afterglow colors under different excitations, anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications were designed. Although many researchers have been engaged in research in this area, these materials often have problems with synthesis complexity, repeatability, and poor process ability. Some organic solvents may also cause environmental pollution [20–22]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop CDs afterglow materials through convenient and environmentally friendly preparation strategies for high-level security protection applications.

Since the CDs we prepared are aggregation-induced burst so we need to add capping materials to ensure better FL and RTP properties. We chose three materials NaOH, H3BO3，cyanuric acid and SiO2 for comparison. It is concluded that NaOH can change the optical band gap of the CDs to regulate their FL properties, and can increase the dispersion and stability of the CDs. While the coating of H3BO3 and SiO2 generally does not significantly change the FL emission of CDs [23,24].

Therefore, this paper synthesized N-doped CDs using sucrose and urea as raw materials, then mixed them into NaOH solution, and embedded the CDs into NaOH crystals through evaporation and crystallization to form a N-CDs@NaOH afterglow material. Through structural characterization and optical property characterization, it was known that the average particle size of N-CDs was 32.5 nm, exhibited blue FL under 365 nm excitation and was excitation-dependent, and had an average lifetime of 8.4 ns. N-CDs@NaOH exhibited blue FL and blue-green RTP under 254 nm excitation, with an average lifetime of 0.82 ms (3 s visible to the naked eye), and blue FL and green RTP under 365 nm excitation, with an average lifetime of 1.56 ms (4 s visible to the naked eye). Based on its different RTP effects at different excitation wavelengths, a multi-level anti-counterfeiting mark controlled by excitation wavelength and lifetime was designed and achieved good results.

Sucrose (Aladdin, China), urea (Aladdin, China), Dimethylformamide (Aladdin, China), Dimethylacetamide (Aladdin, China), and Ethyleneglycol monoethyl ether (DEGME) was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd, China. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. All chemicals used were obtained from commercial sources and directly used without any pre-treatment or further purification. For the purification of the synthesized CDs, a dialysis tubing membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3500 Da from JielePu was used.

The morphology and lateral size of N-CDs were studied using a transmission electron microscope (FEI TECNAI G2 F30). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using Rigaku D/MAX 2500 V from Rigaku Company, Japan. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using the Nicolet IS 50 from Thermo Fisher, USA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using Thermo Fisher Scientific equipment model ESCALAB 250XI+. The laser Raman spectrometer was performed using the inVia Reflex equipment of the British Renishaw Company. The fluorescence spectrometer was performed using equipment model F320 from China Gangdong Technology Co., Ltd. The UV–visible spectrophotometer used Agilent Technologies Co., Ltd. model Agilent 8453 equipment. The highly sensitive and stable transient fluorescence spectrometer was performed using the model FL3C-111 TCSPC of Japan HORIBA Company. The steady-state-transient fluorescence spectrometer was performed using the equipment model OmniFluo990LSP of China Zhuolihanguang Instrument Co., Ltd.

0.68 g sucrose and 0.12 g urea were dissolved into 40 mL deionized (DI) water, transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to solvothermal heating at 180 °C for 5 h, and cooled to room temperature to obtain a dark brown N-CDs solution. The obtained solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microspore syringe, dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 for 48 h, and then freeze-dried to obtain pure N-CDs powder. When the reactant only contained 0.68 g sucrose, the subsequent operations were consistent with the preparation of N-CDs, and the dialyzed CDs solution and pure CDs powder were obtained.

2 mL of dialyzed N-CDs solution and 0.8 g NaOH solid powder were dissolved into 20 mL of DI water, covered the top of the beaker with tin foil, heated at 160 °C until the water evaporates, and grinded the obtained solid into powder to obtain N-CDs@NaOH solid sample, dried and stored. The N-CDs solution was replaced with a CDs solution, and subsequent operations were consistent with the preparation of N-CDs@NaOH to obtain a CDs@NaOH solid sample, which was dried and stored.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the transmission electron microscopy （TEM）image of N-CDs showed that it was evenly distributed. By calculating the lattice stripe spacing in high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), it was found that the lattice spacing was 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite structure [2,25]. At the same time, statistical analysis of its particle size showed that its particle size distribution was mainly between 20 nm and 42 nm, with an average particle size of 32.5 nm (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c, by analyzing the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of N-CDs and N-CDs@NaOH, it could be seen that the XRD curve of N-CDs had an obvious peak at the 25° diffraction angle, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane [26,27], which was consistent with the results observed by HRTEM. In addition, it could be observed that after crystallization coating with NaOH, there was still an obvious broad peak at about 25°, and multiple sharp peaks at the same time, which indicated that N-CDs had successfully achieved crystallization coating with NaOH. As shown in Fig. 1d, through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) testing of both, it could be seen that N-CDs had a broad O–H stretching vibration peak of around 3460 cm−1 and an N–H stretching vibration of around 3128 cm−1 peak [27,28], CO stretching vibration peak around 1639 cm−1 and C–N vibration peak around 1401 cm−1 [29,30], indicating that N-CDs still had functional groups of sucrose and urea. The absorption spectrum of N-CDs@NaOH was similar to that of N-CDs. In addition to the above functional groups, there was a strong absorption region in the range of 3600 cm−1 to 3100 cm−1, corresponding to the O–H group [31], at 2900 cm. There was a weak absorption in the range from 2900 cm−1 to 2700 cm−1, corresponding to the C–H bond [32]. At the same time, there were multiple small absorption peaks in the range from 1500 cm−1 to 500 cm−1, corresponding to the lattice vibration of NaOH. This also indicated that the CDs were uniformly crystalline coated by NaOH.

Subsequently, in order to further explore the elemental and functional group composition of N-CDs, we conducted X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) tests on them. As shown in Fig. 1e, the binding energy peaks of N-CDs were 284.8 eV (C1s), 399 eV (N1s) and 532 eV (O1s). The C element content was 69.81 %, and the N element was 6.64 %, and the O element content was 23.55 %. As shown in Fig. 1f–h, a fine scan of C, O, and N elements showed that C1s was composed of CC/C–C (284.4 eV), C–N (285 eV), C–O (285.9 eV), O–CO (287.8 eV) [33,34], N1s was composed of two parts: pyridine nitrogen (399.2 eV) and pyrrole nitrogen (400.4 eV) [35], while O1s was composed of CO (531.6 eV) and C–O (532.3 eV) consists of two parts [36]. From the above detection, it could be seen that N-CDs were successfully prepared, and their surfaces mainly contained functional groups related to O and N elements. At the same time, N-CDs still had their original functional groups after being mixed with NaOH, and also exhibited the crystal structure of NaOH.

In order to identify the molecular structure and chemical composition of N-CDs, we performed The Raman spectra on them. From Fig. 1i, we could see two obvious peaks, located at 1317 cm−1 and 1577 cm−1 respectively. The peak located at 1317 cm−1 corresponded to the D band of the material, which was usually related to defects and disordered structures caused by impurities in carbon materials [37]. Therefore, the material had a certain degree of disorder and defect structure. The peak at 1577 cm−1 was the G band, which was usually related to the graphitization structure of carbon materials, indicating that the CDs had a certain degree of graphitization, reflecting the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the material, and there were more sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [38]. N-CDs usually introduced defects in carbon materials, and the intensity ratio of D band and G band (ID/IG) was 0.84. Therefore, there might be some level of defects or impurities in the material [39].

In order to understand the optical properties of the prepared samples, we maintained the prepared N-CDs@NaOH under the excitation of 254 nm and 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) light for 8 s respectively. After the UV light was turned off, we found that the samples showed different FL and RTP phenomena.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, when the UV excitation light was 254 nm, the sample FL appeared light blue, and when the UV lamp was turned off, the RTP color appeared blue-green, and when the UV excitation light was 365 nm, the sample FL appeared blue-green, and the RTP color after turning off the UV lamp appeared green. At the same time, we found that under the same UV irradiation time, the RTP duration was about 3 s visible to the naked eye after the 254 nm UV lamp was turned off, while the RTP duration could reach about 4 s visible to the naked eye after the 365 nm UV lamp was turned off.

While optimal excitation wavelength of the N-CDs solution was 470 nm. At this excitation wavelength, it exhibited a green color around 525 nm. At the same time, we could see from Fig. 2b that when the excitation wavelength increased from 330 nm to 510 nm, the FL emission peak of the sample had an excitation-dependent phenomenon, and it gradually changed from blue FL emission to green FL emission. Previous studies had found that it was basically caused by uneven O element defects on the sample surface [40]. In order to explore the relationship between the absorption and excitation of N-CDs, we compared and observed its normalized UV–visible absorption peak, 525 nm excitation peak and 365 nm emission peak, as shown in Fig. 2c. The visible light absorption peaks were mainly at 280 nm and 324 nm, where 280 nm represented the π-π* transition of the conjugated structure and 324 nm represented the n-π* transition dominated by CO or C–N [4,41,42]. The optimal excitation wavelength shown by the excitation spectrum was 468 nm, which was consistent with the emission spectrum. When we used the more common 365 nm as the excitation wavelength, the sample exhibited blue FL emission at 480 nm.

In order to study the FL effect of the crystal-coated sample, we performed excitation scan and emission scan tests on it, as shown in Fig. 2d. The excitation had a peak at 381 nm, and whether it was under 254 nm excitation or under 365 nm excitation, the samples all exhibited blue FL. The emission wavelength when excited at 254 nm was 406 nm, and the emission wavelength under 365 nm excitation was 456 nm, which was not much different from the emission wavelength of N-CDs. The crystal structure of NaOH and surface diffuse reflection might be the reasons for the blue shift of its emission wavelength. As shown in Fig. 2e, the RTP excitation and emission spectra of the sample showed that its optimal excitation wavelength was 338 nm. Under 254 nm excitation, it produced blue RTP emission with a wavelength of 471 nm and a wavelength of 510 nm. The green RTP emission caused it to exhibit blue-green RTP emission, while under 365 nm excitation, the sample only exhibited green RTP emission with a wavelength of about 514 nm. Therefore, the sample showed differences in FL and RTP colors under two different excitation wavelengths.

In order to visually observe the emission centers of N-CDs and N-CDs@NaOH, we conducted three-dimensional FL tests. As shown in Fig. 2f–g, N-CDs exhibited an excitation wavelength of 468 nm and an emission center of 527 nm, while N-CDs@NaOH exhibited two emission centers with an excitation wavelength of 401 nm and an emission wavelength of 477 nm, and an excitation wavelength of 487 nm and an emission wavelength of 562 nm.

In order to explore the effect of mixing N-CDs solute and solvents of different polarities, we carried out fluorescence spectrum detection (Fig. 3 h). We selected four different polar solvents, DI water, Dimethylformamide (DMF), Dimethylacetamide (DMAC), and Diethylene glycol dimethyl ether (DEGME) to explore the relationship between the FL characteristics of N-CDs and polar solutions. When the CDs were dissolved in DI water, they obtained the optimal excitation peak at 450 nm and achieved a blue shift. When the CDs were dissolved in DME, they obtained the optimal excitation peak at 580 nm, achieving a red shift. When the CDs were dissolved in DMAC, they obtained the optimal excitation peak at 560 nm, achieving a slight red shift. When the CDs were dissolved in DEGME, they obtained the optimal excitation peak at 520 nm and achieved a blue shift. In this FL spectrum, we could observe that different solvents had different effects on the FL properties of N-CDs. This was because the solvent molecules form hydrogen bonds with the surface of N-CDs, resulting in different interactions, or the polar solvent changed emission phosphorus spectra the charge distribution or chemical structure of the CDs surface, causing the difference. The polar solvents changed the charge distribution or chemical structure of the CDs surface, causing differences. The polar solvents might reduce the aggregation state of CDs, making their FL emission peaks sharper and more obvious [21].

To explore the effect of N doping on CDs, we used sucrose to prepare CDs sample without N element. We tested the FL properties of CDs, as shown in Fig. 3a. CDs also showed excitation dependence, with an optimal excitation peak of 550 nm obtained under 470 nm excitation, showing green FL. As shown in Fig. 3b, its UV absorption also showed two absorption peaks at 280 nm and 324 nm. The excitation spectrum showed that the best excitation wavelength was 470 nm, which was consistent with the FL spectrum. The sample exhibited 500 nm which was approximately blue-green FL under 365 nm excitation. Its three-dimensional FL spectrum was shown in Fig. 3c. Its emission center was an excitation wavelength of 470 nm and an emission wavelength of 552 nm. Subsequently, we took photos of CDs@NaOH under UV light and found (Fig. 3d) that the RTP duration was only about 2 s, which was significantly shorter than the RTP duration of N-CDs@NaOH, which showed that the N doping improved the hydrogen bond donors and acceptors of CDs and enhanced the RTP effect of the sample.

As shown in Fig. 3e–g, it could be seen from its XPS spectrum that, except that it did not contain N elements, the components of CDs were not much different from N-CDs, and their binding energy peaks were 284.8 eV (C1s) and 532 eV (O1s). The C element content was 74.67 %, and the O element content was 25.33 %. After careful scanning, it was found that C1s was only composed of three parts which was C–C/CC (284.5 eV), C–O (285.8 eV), and O–CO (288.3 eV), while O1s was similar to N-CDs, also containing two parts which were CO (531.4 eV) and C–O (532.9 eV).

We then measured the Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the sample. Through the test, we found that the PLQY of N-CDs in the liquid state was 3.97 %, while the PLQY of the N-CDs@NaOH solid sample was 3.4 %. There was not much reduction, which indicated that the solid structure after crystal coating hindered the occurrence of aggregation-induced quenching of N-CDs to a certain extent.

As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, we knew by fitting the quadratic term of the life curves of N-CDs and N-CDs@NaOH that the average lifetime τaverage of N-CDs in the liquid state was 8.4 ns, and The average lifetime τaverage of solid samples under 254 nm excitation was 0.82 ms, and the average lifetime τaverage of solid samples under 365 nm excitation was 1.56 ms. It could be seen that the average lifetime under 365 nm excitation was higher than that under 254 nm excitation, which was consistent with the duration of RTP seen by the naked eye on the sample.

As shown in Fig. 4c, N-CDs were crystal-coated with NaOH in order to structurally restrict the N-CDs through the unique crystal structure of NaOH to suppress its spin singlet state and radiation transition process, and make RTP produced by intersystem crossing (ISC) to the spin triplet state. As shown in Fig. 4d, generally speaking, the oxygen-containing groups of CDs were formed by local defects on their surfaces, producing different energy levels in the π-π* gap [43–45]. When CDs were excited by excitation light of different external excitation wavelengths, and electrons were excited from the ground state (π) to different vibration energy levels of the excited state (π*), most of the excited electrons were captured by the defect state related to O. Since only non-radiative transitions could occur between different energy levels within O-related defects, the FL between each energy level was different, resulting in excitation-dependent FL emission [46–48].

The sample after mixing and crystallization was limited by the NaOH crystal structure. After the excitation stopped, the radiative transition was limited. Some of the excited electrons went thought spin reversal in the excited state, and an ISC occurred between the excited singlet state and the excited triplet state. N-CDs jumped to the excited triplet state, then reached the lowest vibrational energy level through vibrational relaxation, and finally jumped the photon to the vibrational energy level of the ground state in the form of a radiative transition, producing a RTP effect [49].

Finally, we used the obtained N-CDs@NaOH sample and designed a series of anti-counterfeiting marks to obtain better anti-counterfeiting effects. As shown in Fig. 5 a, at first, we bonded the powder on the paper to form the “GXU” letter pattern. When under natural light conditions, we can found that it appeared pure white. When we used a 254 nm UV lamp to illuminate the mark, it could be found that the sample appeared blue light, and when the UV lamp stopped irradiating, the sample showed a blue-green RTP effect. When we used a 365 nm UV lamp to illuminate the mark, the sample showed a blue FL effect. When the UV lamp stopped irradiating, the sample showed a green RTP effect. At the same time, we found that when the UV lamp stopped irradiating for 4 s, the label irradiated by the 365 nm UV lamp could still show the RTP effect, while the label irradiated by the 254 nm UV lamp lost the RTP effect.

Then we used the N-CDs@NaOH sample together with the CDs@NaOH sample, as shown in Fig. 5b. When under natural light conditions, both were pure white, and there were not much difference in appearance, and showed two numbers “88”. When 254 nm and 365 nm UV light irradiated the sample, the FL of CDs@NaOH turned white, thus rendering the number “24”. When the UV lamp was turned off, the number “88″ appeared again, because the RTP colors of the two were relatively close. Since the RTP time of CDs@NaOH was shorter, the number “24” appeared again in the sample after a period of time, and finally the number disappeared until the RTP disappeared. This kind of information anti-counterfeiting method in which information was closed to opened and then closed and then opened again, could greatly improve the anti-counterfeiting effect. At the same time, we found that the mixed powder still maintained its original FL and RTP effect after being stored in a dry environment for two months, indicating that it had good stability and had great application prospects.

In this paper, N-CDs with excitation-dependence were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using sucrose as raw material. Through a series of characterization methods such as structural characterization and optical property characterization, it was found that the PLQY of N-CDs was 3.97 %, and the optimal excitation wavelength were 470 nm and 365 nm, exhibited green FL of 525 nm under 470 nm excitation and blue FL of 480 nm under 365 nm excitation, respectively. The N-CDs@NaOH sample exhibited blue FL emissions of 406 nm and 465 nm under excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively. And there was blue-green RTP emission of 471 nm and 510 nm after excitation at 254 nm, with a lifetime of 0.82 ms (visible to the naked eye for 3 s). But after excitation at 365 nm, it only exhibited green RTP emission of 514 nm, with a lifetime of 1.56 ms (visible to the naked eye for 4 s). An anti-counterfeiting mark was designed using N-CDs@NaOH to exhibit different FL and RTP effects at different excitation wavelengths. It not only had high recognition, but also had good stability even after being stored under natural conditions for two months. Afterwards, its FL and RTP effect still had not changed much.

This technology not only had the advantages of low cost and easy operation, but also could achieve RTP excitation. Therefore, the multi-level anti-counterfeiting technology based on N-CDs will provide an innovative and feasible solution to improve product security and lay a solid foundation for research and application in related fields.

Jinkun Xue: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiang Mao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xia Zhao: Software, Formal analysis. Ting Xie: Writing – review & editing. Hao Hu: Data curation. Zequan Li: Writing – review & editing. Wei Gao: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Guangxi Science and Technology Program (AD23026201); Key R&D projects in Guangxi (AB22080040); Industry-university Research Cooperation Fund of Guangxi Fucheng Furniture Group Co., Ltd & Guangxi University (20200750).
